reviews.review_text:
There's a lot of great stuff here but it's all weighed down by how much this game wants to waste your time. 

A single run in this game could take you 40 real hours, so if something unexpected happens to ruin your run 30 hours in, it would take you 30 hours to start a new game and get back to where you left off.

I feel like one of the definitive qualities of a good indie title is how weird it is willing to get.  Cultist Simulator is different than anything else on the market, to the point where it's hard to point out a genre.  This one is for the loreheads, assuming the loreheads also like stress, random and permanent deaths, and hours upon hours of lost progress for failing memory and attention mechanics (I will point you towards Book of Hours for a *substantially* friendlier sequel that does not require a Cultist Sim victory to play)

Without going too deep into its structure, you have some nouns and some adjectives and a slightly increasing selection of verbs, and your goal is to assemble them into coherent sentences before finally determining just what the hell it is you are reading.  Time is a factor but not an overly critical one, ticked away in minute intervals that feel unnecessary at first but soon serve to provide you breathing space to respond to crises, form strategems, break out of your routines and into new ones, and just live in your brain for a bit.  

As the game unfolds into a mass of ideas and a mess of cards, a narrative about investigating and understanding the occult transpires, while juggling waning sanity, looming conspiracy and a grind of a day job.  It's really good, in the spirit and ancestry of the Fallen London/Sunless Seas games in its humor, darkness and esoterica - and like them it is a game with almost no graphics, just gentle abstractions and some text dripping with texture and flavor.

When its good, the mechanics inform the narrative, when its bad, the narrative still prevails - though sometimes you might feel compelled to go wiki-diving to figure out how to read on without dying.  I wouldn't say Cultist Simulator is a finished thought in all its respects (particularly as a prelude to Book of Hours, a different but similar follow-up with substantially less maddening stress and fatality) but it is entertainingly broken. If you come in it with a desire to just investigate the world from a position of innocence (Disco style), and uncover a world in fragments to piece it together as a whole (Dark Souls style), you're likely to have a good time.

About the game, it's tideously long, more than it probably should be, it's methodical. It claims to make you discover things, not in reality you are mad man, trying to hit every wall, untill you either gain enough prespective to really see things, or stumble unpon them without realization. It feels longer than life, it excites me by it's mysteries, but at the end of the day it spells out for you what you need to do, you just need to know on every door to see what you really need to win. At the end the only reason you stay is obsession. If you don't feel it, I would not recommend to buy

Oh what I would give to play this game for the first time again and experience its mysteries. I recommend this game to everyone I can, even though it might not be their type of game, because I didn’t know it was my type of game. The writing is incredible, there is so much feeling stuffed inside every line from a description of some trash to a book describing how to summon monsters. Every word grips my imagination and yanks me into this world.
The gameplay really sells the feeling of trying to juggle so many things at once, and with no tutorials, reading and experimenting are your friends, just like how some up and coming cultist would do. For anyone playing for the first time I would recommend going in blind but if you get stuck, try asking someone who has played it before for a hint instead of looking up a guide.
I first found this game on iOS in high school (it was apparently my senior year when this game out, though I feel like I played it all throughout high school) and I had absolutely no idea what this game was. I didn’t beat it for years but all along I could catch the glimpses of what existed between the cracks in its world, and I kept coming back to try again.
Years later I installed the game on vacation and got so much further than ever before, I really should have put it down and done more but I just couldn’t. That was when I found that they had recently released a second game, so I was excited to come back to my PC and play it. Since then this world has only dragged me deeper into it.

Having now, after 54 hours, finally reached my first "proper" ending, and embarking on a new run:

This is not a game for everyone. But what it does, it does extremely well.

At its core are two elements - Worldbuilding and Discovery

The world and lore feel organic, in a way where the occult concepts feel like an integral part of its reality, not a tacked-on "magic system". The snippets of lore weave together a truly unique atmosphere. Add a healthy bit of an unreliable (?) narrator feeling, and I must say I have never experienced something so (to me) engrossing in a game world.

Now, the gameplay. The thing about this game is that it is a roguelike at heart. It may feel like a "wiki game" but I would suggest refraining from the sweet allure of guides and looking up anything beyond maybe the basic controls. Take chances. Take notes. Every time you fail, you will return wiser and  more prepared. The notes you take are the meta progression. Losing is fun. Winning feels earned.

if you love to play a game where you don't ever have a clue what's going on, cultist simulator might be for you. I've sunk hours into this game without a singular glimmer of understanding. if there is a sprecific goal or order of operations, I sure can't tell. somehow I'm getting achievements for things I supposedly achieved in game that I can't make heads or tails of. maddening.
Got me through College finals and into Fallen London so it automatically deserves some points there

I played two runs, and honestly, I feel like I accomplished nothing and learned nothing from it (if it wasn't for looking at the wiki, maybe it would be literally nothing, you 100% need a wiki to play this, and it will probably spoil some things that will take some fun off of it). I don't feel like I want to play it again.

The atmosphere and writing are incredible, you really want to get into the cult and mysticism and learn the hidden truths. The problem is the game teaches you absolutely nothing and throws dangers at you that could end your run all the time. It's like the game is teasing you with everything it contains and then gatekeeping you out of it. It's not inviting, it does not make you want to play more to finally achieve something, it just makes you feel like you wasted 2 hours of your life in a run that ultimately amounted to nothing.

I don't know man, it left a bad taste in my mouth, very bittersweet, I loved the Lovecraftian atmosphere, and I want to know about the possible endings, and read the texts of the events and stuff, but I don't want to engage with the game's mechanics. Maybe I'll watch someone play it on youtube, or maybe I'll change my mind and try again, but for now I don't feel like recommending this to others.

I rate it as ok. More of a cryptic puzzle than a game. After 15 ours I still don't have it figured out. Gameplay gets me hooked but I have to be in a certain mood (am I ok with being confused today) to get the urge to play. Worth a try at the very least. Just my 2 cents

Ok so, this is an interesting game. The lore and vibes are top tier, you get really invested in your own little narrative about who and what your character is doing and the way the world unveils itself over time into this cosmic eldritch fantasy is awesome

But the gameplay is... well its really binary in splitting opinions. I liked it, at least for a few times. Some people cannot stand it and I get that. If you're questioning whether its a game for you, try it for a while and if you find yourself getting hooked, it only gets better. If you think its incredibly tedious and slow, then thats your answer

Seek the truths beneath the skin of the world.

This is a hard and esoteric game that does NOT teach you how to play it. You have to earn that knowledge through trial and error.

I'm going to try to keep this review fairly short.  I enjoyed Cultist Simulator a fair bit and think it's worth playing.  That said, this game takes way more time to play then it feels like it should.  Every time I consider starting another playthrough, I remember just how long my previous ones were and decide against it, especially when I have other games to play.  I'd rate it 7/10.

To be honest, I feel like I'm losing my mind playing this game. In a really good way. The confusion, stress, and searching for clues perfectly mirrors the content of this game. Very excited to play the sequel.
This game is simply amazing. One of those gems that reinvent a genre. Don't look for guides or clues. Just play, discover and enjoy the trip

It's too confusing with too much going on at the start. 

Ive tried this way too many times and havent even slightly made progress
Game is fine. Played it a lot on my phone. But for some reason I keep getting adds for an 8 year old game all of a sudden.  Leave me alone
IN THE DESERT I WAIT IN THE RUINS I BURN - METAL IS WATER - STONE IS WAX - FLESH IS SMOKE - ENTER ME AND BE NO LONGER.
when i close my eyes i see the cards. my mind has molded itself to the gameplay. 10/10 would recommend to someone who has free time and also nothing to lose
It's fun, and extremely well-written. The game sets a strong tone, and plays with it very well. I'll say it's not for everyone, but for those who are hit by it, are hit hard.
The hour glass is my worst enemy
Wonderful game for a ghoul.
enjoyable
coltist
great
